description: JVM runtime dashboard for Java services auto-instrumented by the OpenTelemetry Operator (autoinstrumentation-java:2.26.1). Covers CPU, memory (heap/non-heap pools), garbage collection, threads, and classes.

links.title: OpenTelemetry JVM Metrics Spec
links.tooltip: Semantic conventions for JVM runtime metrics

panels.description: Number of unique pods currently reporting JVM metrics within the selected filters.
panels.title: Pods Reporting

panels.description: Number of distinct services (service.name) reporting within the selected filters.
panels.title: Services

panels.description: Recent CPU utilization averaged across selected pods. Based on jvm.cpu.recent_utilization (via getProcessCpuLoad()) — bounded [0,1] where 1.0 means the JVM saturates all CPUs available to the container.
panels.title: Avg CPU Utilization

panels.description: Sum of heap memory used across selected pods.
panels.title: Total Heap Used

panels.description: Sum of platform threads across selected pods.
panels.title: Total Threads

panels.description: Sum of classes currently loaded across selected pods.
panels.title: Classes Loaded

panels.description: Recent CPU utilization per pod as reported by the JVM (jvm.cpu.recent_utilization, via getProcessCpuLoad()). Bounded [0,1]; 1.0 means the JVM saturates all CPUs available to the container, not just one core.
panels.title: CPU Recent Utilization

panels.description: Per-core CPU utilization computed as rate(jvm.cpu.time) / jvm.cpu.count, averaged over the rate interval. Same [0,1] scale as Panel 10 (CPU Recent Utilization), but Panel 10 reports the JVM's instantaneous self-reported load while this one is a smoother windowed average — compare the two to spot transient spikes.
panels.title: CPU Per-Core Utilization (from jvm.cpu.time)

panels.description: Heap memory used broken down by heap pool (Eden Space, Survivor Space, Tenured Gen / Old Gen). Each (pod, pool) is one stacked series — for clearest view, filter to a single pod via the $pod variable.
panels.title: Heap Used by Pool

panels.description: Total heap memory: used vs committed vs limit. The gap between used and committed is reclaimable by the JVM; the gap between committed and limit is available for growth.
panels.title: Heap Used vs Committed vs Limit

panels.description: Heap utilization: used / limit (percentage). Sustained values above 85% often indicate memory pressure; consistent near-100% after GCs can signal a leak. Note: for Serial/Parallel GC the per-pool max sum (Eden + Survivor + Tenured) can exceed -Xmx because Young pools share space, so values may slightly underestimate at saturation.
panels.title: Heap Utilization %

panels.description: Heap memory used immediately after the most recent GC cycle, per pool. The baseline of live data after collection — a steadily rising Tenured/Old Gen baseline is the clearest signal of a memory leak.
panels.title: Heap Used After Last GC (by Pool)

panels.description: Non-heap memory used by pool (Metaspace, Code Cache / CodeHeap segments, Compressed Class Space). Metaspace growth reflects classloader retention; unbounded growth signals a classloader leak. Pool names differ across JDK versions (JDK 8 reports a single 'Code Cache'; JDK 9+ reports three 'CodeHeap' segments) — filter to one pod for a clean view.
panels.title: Non-Heap Used by Pool

panels.description: Non-heap committed vs used. Note: Metaspace has no upper limit metric (JVM reports it unbounded), so only committed is shown as the effective ceiling.
panels.title: Non-Heap Used vs Committed

panels.description: GC cycles per second by collector and action. 'Copy' / 'end of minor GC' = young-gen collections (fast, frequent). 'MarkSweepCompact' / 'end of major GC' = old-gen collections (slow, infrequent). Spikes in major GC rate warrant investigation.
panels.title: GC Rate (ops/s)

panels.description: GC pause duration percentiles from the jvm_gc_duration histogram. P99 shows worst-case pauses users see. Watch for growth in major GC P99 — that's stop-the-world pause length.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.50, sum by (le, k8s_pod_name, jvm_gc_name, jvm_gc_action) (rate(jvm_gc_duration_bucket{k8s_namespace_name="$k8s_namespace",service_name=~"$service",k8s_pod_name=~"$pod"}[$__rate_interval])))

histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, k8s_pod_name, jvm_gc_name, jvm_gc_action) (rate(jvm_gc_duration_bucket{k8s_namespace_name="$k8s_namespace",service_name=~"$service",k8s_pod_name=~"$pod"}[$__rate_interval])))

histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, k8s_pod_name, jvm_gc_name, jvm_gc_action) (rate(jvm_gc_duration_bucket{k8s_namespace_name="$k8s_namespace",service_name=~"$service",k8s_pod_name=~"$pod"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: GC Duration Percentiles

panels.description: Mean GC duration = sum(rate(duration_sum)) / sum(rate(duration_count)). Smooths percentile jitter and gives a stable feel for typical pause cost.

targets.expr: sum by (k8s_pod_name, jvm_gc_name, jvm_gc_action) (rate(jvm_gc_duration_sum{k8s_namespace_name="$k8s_namespace",service_name=~"$service",k8s_pod_name=~"$pod"}[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (k8s_pod_name, jvm_gc_name, jvm_gc_action) (rate(jvm_gc_duration_count{k8s_namespace_name="$k8s_namespace",service_name=~"$service",k8s_pod_name=~"$pod"}[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: GC Mean Duration

panels.description: Percentage of wall time spent in GC = rate(duration_sum) × 100. Sustained values above ~5% indicate GC pressure; above 10% is usually a problem.
panels.title: GC Time %

panels.description: Platform thread count broken down by state. Stable states (runnable / waiting / timed_waiting) are reported in steady state; transient states (blocked / new / terminated) typically do not appear because they last only briefly. Rising runnable with flat throughput = CPU contention. Rising blocked = lock contention.
panels.title: Threads by State

panels.description: Daemon vs non-daemon (user) thread counts. Non-daemon threads keep the JVM alive; unbounded non-daemon growth is a red flag.
panels.title: Threads: Daemon vs Non-Daemon

panels.description: Classes currently loaded (jvm.class.count). A slowly growing trend is normal during warmup; steady growth over hours indicates a classloader leak.
panels.title: Classes Loaded (current)

panels.description: Rate of class loading (jvm.class.loaded per second). High rate outside startup may indicate dynamic class generation (e.g., proxies, scripting).
panels.title: Class Load Rate

panels.description: Rate of class unloading (jvm.class.unloaded per second). Typically near zero; sustained non-zero values accompany classloader churn.
panels.title: Class Unload Rate

title: ACP OpenTelemetry JVM
